**[Section 1 --- Purpose & Scope]{.underline}**

**[1.1 --- Purpose]{.underline}**

The purpose of this Playbook is to provide Meridian Health Systems, Inc. (\"Meridian\" or the \"Company\") negotiation teams with standardized positions, fallback ladders, red lines, and board-approved risk tolerances for procuring AI-enabled and advanced technology solutions. As Meridian continues to expand its deployment of artificial intelligence across clinical decision-support, patient-facing applications, and operational workflows, the Office of General Counsel has determined that a unified, disciplined approach to vendor negotiations is essential to protect the Company\'s clinical, financial, and reputational interests.

This Playbook is not merely advisory. It establishes the authoritative framework within which all AI and advanced technology vendor negotiations must be conducted. Negotiation teams are required to adhere to the positions, fallback ladders, and escalation procedures described herein. Deviations from this Playbook are permitted only through the escalation mechanisms described in Section 12.

**[1.2 --- Intended Audience]{.underline}**

This Playbook is intended for use by the following authorized personnel:

> • **Office of General Counsel**: Dr. Priya Narayanan, SVP & General Counsel, and all attorneys within the Meridian Legal Department assigned to technology transactions.
>
> • **Digital Health & Technology Transactions**: Marcus Delgado, VP, and all procurement professionals and contract managers within the Digital Health & Technology Transactions team.
>
> • **Authorized Outside Counsel**: Whitfield Ames LLP, specifically Elaine Sutter, Partner, and members of her team assigned to Meridian engagements.
>
> • **Designated Procurement Leads**: Any procurement professional authorized by Marcus Delgado to participate in AI/technology vendor negotiations on behalf of Meridian.

No other Meridian personnel are authorized to access or rely upon this Playbook without the express written consent of Dr. Priya Narayanan.

**[1.3 --- Scope of Application]{.underline}**

This Playbook applies to all AI/technology vendor agreements where any one or more of the following criteria are met:

> \(a\) The agreement involves the deployment of **clinical decision-support or patient-facing AI** systems, including but not limited to sepsis prediction models, radiology triage algorithms, medication-interaction alert systems, natural language processing tools for clinical documentation, and any AI system that generates outputs used by clinicians in the course of patient care.
>

**[1. Liability Framework]{.underline}**

The following sections describe Cognitive Foundry\'s standard approach to liability allocation across its enterprise healthcare deployments. Cognitive Foundry considers a well-defined and appropriately calibrated liability framework to be foundational to sustainable, long-term partnerships in the healthcare AI space. The Company\'s positions on liability reflect not only its own risk management posture but also the broader ecosystem considerations that arise when a single vendor\'s platform is deployed across dozens of health systems, each with distinct patient populations, clinical workflows, and regulatory obligations.

**1.1 General Liability Cap**

Under Cognitive Foundry\'s standard contract terms, the aggregate liability of each party for all claims arising under or in connection with the agreement --- whether in contract, tort (including negligence), strict liability, or any other legal or equitable theory --- is capped at an amount equal to one times (1×) the trailing twelve-month fees paid or payable by Customer under the agreement. For purposes of this calculation, \"trailing twelve-month fees\" means the total fees actually paid by Customer, together with all fees that have accrued and remain payable, during the twelve (12) month period immediately preceding the event giving rise to the claim, or, if the agreement has been in effect for less than twelve (12) months, the annualized value of the fees paid or payable during the period the agreement has been in effect.

For a contract of the size and scope currently contemplated in connection with the proposed Meridian engagement, the general liability cap would be approximately \$9.6 million, based on an anticipated annual contract value (\"ACV\") of \$9.6 million per year. This figure is derived from Meridian\'s preliminary deployment plan covering fourteen (14) acute care facilities and associated ambulatory sites, and is subject to adjustment based on the final scope of the deployment as set forth in the applicable Order Form.

The general liability cap applies to each party equally and is intended to provide a balanced allocation of risk that is proportionate to the economic value of the agreement. Cognitive Foundry applies this cap consistently across its enterprise customer base and considers it to be appropriate for contracts of this magnitude and nature.

**1.2 Super-Cap Carve-Outs**

Cognitive Foundry recognizes that certain categories of liability warrant heightened exposure beyond the general cap. """Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

**Net.** This is a Priority #3 term where Meridian is making a real concession (the graduated ETF) in exchange for the Vendor's acceptance of a coherent, clinically-safe exit package. The package should be presented as a unit; the ETF concession is the lever, the transition/data terms are the patient-safety and operational protections.

### (iv) Regulatory and Compliance Constraints

-   **HIPAA / HITECH.** The BAA (Exhibit F) governs PHI return/destruction upon termination; §14.6 must be coordinated with the BAA's return-or-destroy obligation. The 45-day timeline is consistent with the BAA framework. Certified destruction per NIST SP 800-88 satisfies the BAA's destruction standard.
-   **NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act.** The transition and data-return provisions must preserve Meridian's ability to maintain audit records and transparency documentation for the NJ DOH (15-business-day production requirement) following termination. The continued-obligations provision (§14.5(d)) keeps Article 10 (audit/transparency) in effect during the Transition Period. The no-data-hostage provision ensures Meridian can comply with regulatory document-production obligations regardless of fee disputes.
-   **FDA.** If ClinIQ™ is FDA-regulated, transition obligations must accommodate any FDA-mandated wind-down or patient-notification requirements. The change-of-law provision (Playbook §10.4) and the 18-month transition period provide adequate runway.
-   **Data portability (Playbook §2.4).** FHIR R4 and HL7 v2 export is a Playbook red line and a MeridianConnect (Epic) interoperability requirement. The 45-day timeline with both formats satisfies this. Data portability obligations survive termination.

## Term 4 --- Aggregated and De-Identified Data Usage Rights

**Priority rank: 4 of 7 \| Agreement sections: §5.1--5.4 \| Related: Exhibit D (Data Governance), Exhibit F (BAA)**

### (i) Proposed Contract Clause Language

The following language replaces §5.4 of the Vendor draft and resolves the contested bracketed alternatives in Exhibit D §5.2. It is redline-ready.

**§5.4 Aggregated and De-Identified Data Use.**

(a) **Permitted Use.** Subject to Vendor's compliance with the de-identification standards set forth in Section 5.3, Vendor may use Aggregated Data and De-Identified Data (collectively, "Derived Data") derived from Customer's use of the Platform solely for the following purposes: (i) improving, training, fine-tuning, validating, and enhancing the Platform and Vendor's AI/ML models; and (ii) internal benchmarking of model performance. For the avoidance of doubt, Derived Data has been de-identified in compliance with 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(a)--(c) and does not constitute PHI.

(b) **Prohibited Uses.** Vendor shall not (i) sell, license, sublicense, rent, or distribute Derived Data to any third party for commercial purposes; (ii) use Derived Data to develop or enhance products unrelated to the Platform or Vendor's clinical AI product line; or (iii) use Derived Data for marketing or competitive analysis of Customer. Vendor may share Derived Data with subprocessors solely for the purposes described in subsection (a), subject to confidentiality obligations no less protective than Article 11.

(c) **Opt-Out Right.** Customer may elect to opt out of Vendor's use of Derived Data under subsection (a) at any time by providing written notice to Vendor. The opt-out shall be effective thirty (30) days following Vendor's receipt of such notice and is not subject to any cure period, notice of deficiency, or Vendor consent. Customer may exercise the opt-out for any reason or no reason.

(d) **Effect of Opt-Out --- Deletion.** Upon the effective date of an opt-out notice, Vendor shall: (i) cease all new use of Customer-derived Derived Data on a prospective basis; and (ii) within sixty (60) days, perform verified deletion of all Customer-derived Derived Data that has not yet been incorporated into trained models, and certify such deletion in writing, signed by an officer of Vendor, within five (5) business days of completion. With respect to Customer-derived Derived Data that has already been incorporated into trained models or derivative works as of the opt-out effective date, Vendor shall, within six (6) months of the opt-out effective date, either (A) retrain the affected models without Customer-derived Derived Data and certify that the contribution of such data has been eliminated, or (B) if Vendor demonstrates to Customer's reasonable satisfaction that retraining is technically infeasible, implement and certify alternative isolation measures (including, where applicable, suppression of Customer-specific model adaptations) that achieve equivalent protection. The burden of demonstrating technical infeasibility rests with Vendor.

(e) **Transparency and Documentation.** Vendor shall maintain records identifying the sources and categories of Derived Data used for model improvement and benchmarking, and shall provide Customer with an annual summary report (no later than March 31 of each year) describing the categories of use to which Customer-derived Derived Data has been applied. Vendor shall update training-data provenance documentation to reflect any retraining that incorporates Customer-derived Derived Data, consistent with the transparency obligations of Section 10.1 and the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act.

(f) **No Implied License.** Nothing in this Agreement grants Vendor any implied license or right to use Customer Data, Aggregated Data, or De-Identified Data except as expressly set forth in this Section 5.4. Vendor's rights under this Section 5.4 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement solely with respect to Derived Data that Vendor is permitted to retain under subsection (d).
